topic: IO - Seek Trait and SeekFrom Variants

turns.content: I have a file in memory using a Vec<u8> wrapped in a Cursor, and I'm currently seeking to the end of the data with seek(SeekFrom::End(0)) before reading it backwards. Is this actually better than my current approach?

Using SeekFrom::End is perfectly valid for moving to the last byte, but your logic about seeking backwards suggests you might be confusing how seek positions work. The seek method returns a u32 indicating the new position immediately after the operation. When you call seek(SeekFrom::End(n)), the value n is added to the current offset, not treated as an absolute coordinate. If you want to start from the beginning regardless of where you are, calling rewind() on your Cursor is the idiomatic way to reset its internal file pointer without needing to calculate offsets manually.
Ah, so I don't need to do any math? But what if I want to read from the middle of a stream and then continue reading forward?

That is exactly why SeekFrom::Current exists. If you seek(SeekFrom::Current(n)), it adds n bytes to your existing file position without resetting everything. You can read some data, use seek(SeekFrom::Current(-n)) to go back by n bytes, and then seek(SeekFrom::Start(m)) if you need an absolute jump from zero. This combination lets you implement a tee-style reader where you buffer the first half of a stream, reset via rewind(), read the second half, then calculate how much was buffered using the final position.
I see. So for my interview question about memory-mapped files, should I use SeekFrom::Start instead of calculating offsets?

For memory-mapped files accessed via a Cursor, you should prefer the relative variants like Current and End unless you specifically need absolute positioning. The Seek trait guarantees that seek returns the new position, so you can chain operations safely without knowing your starting offset. If you calculate offsets manually using raw indices on the Vec<u8>, you bypass the safety checks of the std::io module entirely. Using Cursor with SeekFrom variants keeps your code robust against off-by-one errors.
One more thing, how do I check if a stream is actually seekable before using these methods?

You implement the Seek trait on your type. If you are wrapping a Vec<u8> in a Cursor, it implements Seek automatically because Vec has random access capabilities. However, if you were working with a file handle without seeking support, calling seek would panic unless you first checked the trait bounds or used stream_position to verify the current offset before attempting any movement operations.
Got it. So for my implementation of a log parser, I'll use SeekFrom::Start(0) to reset after each chunk?

Yes, that is the correct pattern for your log parser. You read a fixed-size chunk, then call seek(SeekFrom::Current(-size)) to move back by the exact number of bytes you just consumed. This allows you to process overlapping windows or re-examine specific lines without loading the entire file into memory again.
